question: I just found this course late — can I still join now, or is it too late to start?
answer_llm:
Yes, you can still join now. You can start learning anytime.

If you want a certificate, make sure to submit your project while submissions are still open.

question: If I join after the course has already started, am I still able to get a certificate?

question: What do I need to do to qualify for a certificate if I join the course late?
answer_llm:
To qualify for a certificate after joining late, you need to:

- submit your project while the course is still accepting submissions
- finish the course with the live cohort
- peer-review 3 capstones after submitting your project

Homework is not required for the certificate, but you must pass the capstone project.
answer_orig: Yes, but if you want to receive a certificate, you need to submit your project while we’re still accepting submissions.

question: Is it okay to start the course after it begins, and does that affect the final project submission?
answer_llm:
Yes, you can start whenever you want, even after the course has begun.

For the final project, the key point is that the capstone submission happens within the course deadlines and peer-review window. If you miss the first submission window, you can still catch up for the attempt #2 window.

So starting late does not automatically prevent you from submitting the final project, but you still need to submit during the available course windows and complete peer review of 3 peers to get the certificate.

question: How do I join the Office Hours or live workshop stream if I’m a student?
answer_llm:

- Watch the stream on the **DataTalksClub YouTube Channel**
- Use **Slido** for questions while live
- Check the **announcements channel on Telegram or Slack** for the video URL before the session starts
- Don’t post questions in chat, since they may be missed

question: How do I find my own entry on the leaderboard if I can’t spot my name right away?
answer_llm: Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link — that takes you directly to your entry, even if you can’t spot your name right away.
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Why do I show up with a random name on the leaderboard instead of my real one?
answer_llm:
You show up with a random name because the course automatically assigns you an anonymous display name when your account is created.

That random name is what appears on the public leaderboard by default. If you want to change it, go to **Edit Course Profile** and update the **first field** — that is your public nickname/display name.

Your **real name** goes in the **second field**, which is used for your certificate and not shown publicly.

question: Why does the homework content keep changing before I can submit it?
answer_llm:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is only ready when both are true:
- the homework form is open on the course management platform, and
- the homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on it before that is at your own risk, because the final version may be different.

question: Is the name I enter for the certificate visible on the public leaderboard or anywhere else online?
answer_llm: No. The certificate name only appears on your certificate and is not published online or shared with third parties. The public leaderboard uses a separate display name/nickname instead.
answer_orig:
No. The certificate name only appears on your certificate — it isn't published online or shared with third parties. The public leaderboard uses your separate display name instead, which is a random nickname (like “Lucid Elbakyan”) and anonymous by default.

You can set both in “Edit Course Profile”: the first field is your public nickname for the leaderboard, and the second is the official name printed on your certificate. This lets you keep your real name off the public leaderboard while still having it on the certificate.

question: Why doesn't this course use LangChain right away?
answer_llm:
Because the course wants you to learn the **core RAG building blocks first**—prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation—before adding a framework like LangChain.

The idea is similar to learning **HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before React or Angular**: frameworks are much easier to use well once you understand what they automate.
answer_orig:
LangChain is a framework for building LLM-powered apps. In this course, we first build the core pieces ourselves: prompting, retrieval, indexing, and evaluation.

Think of it like learning HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before using React or Angular. Frameworks are easier to use well once you understand what they automate.

question: Why do I get an OpenAI API error saying I have insufficient credits when I run responses.create?
answer_llm:
You’re seeing that error because your OpenAI API account has **insufficient quota/credits**. The course context says there is **no free usage of the OpenAI API**, so `responses.create` will fail until you add billing credits.

What to do:
1. **Add credits** to your OpenAI account billing (minimum about **$5**).
2. Make sure you’re using a **model available to your account** in `responses.create(model='gpt-4o', ...)`.
3. If needed, **create a new API key** after adding credits and update it locally.

question: Why does the course recommend uv over pip and virtualenv for setup?
answer_llm:
The course recommends `uv` because it’s **fast, convenient, and all-in-one**. It replaces several traditional tools like `pip` and `virtualenv`, so instead of managing Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies separately, you can do it with one fast command and reproducible lockfiles.

That said, you **don’t have to use it** — `pip` and `virtualenv` still work fine. The main reason the lessons use `uv` is that it makes setup simpler and faster.
answer_orig:
The course uses uv because it's fast and convenient. It's an all-in-one tool that replaces pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine — instead of juggling several tools, you manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies through one fast command with reproducible lockfiles.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

- **One tool instead of many**

- **Reproducible environments**

from dotenv import load_dotenv

    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)
```

question: How do I keep my API keys in a .env file and load them in Python?
answer_llm:
Store your keys in a `.env` file and load them with `python-dotenv`.

1. Add `.env` to `.gitignore`:
```gitignore
.env
```

2. Create a `.env` file:
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

3. Install `python-dotenv` if needed:
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

4. Load the variables in Python:
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

If you're using a Jupyter notebook, make sure to call `load_dotenv()` before creating the client.

question: What’s the setup for storing API secrets locally without committing them to Git?
answer_llm:
Use `dirdotenv` to load secrets from a local `.env` file automatically, and keep that file out of Git.

Example setup:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# in your project
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

If you already use `direnv`, that also works: put the exports in `.envrc`, run `direnv allow`, and make sure `.envrc` is also in `.gitignore`.

For Python scripts, you can also load the local `.env` with `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()
```

question: How can I use Gemini in the Module 1 notebook through the OpenAI-style endpoint, and what do I need to set up first?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI-compatible endpoint with Google Gemini by:

1. Saving your Gemini API key in `.env` as something like `GEMINI_API_KEY`
2. Creating an OpenAI client pointed at Google’s base URL
3. Passing the Gemini model name in your request

Example setup from Module 1:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    message_history = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=message_history
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

So first you need:
- a Gemini API key in your `.env`
- the OpenAI client configured with the Gemini base URL
- the Gemini model name you want to use

Also note: this uses the older `chat.completions` style through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so you may need to adjust notebook code where it reads responses or handles tools/function calls.

Example code (loads the API key from `.env`, creates the Gemini client, and defines the `llm` helper):

```python

That means you will need to change the notebook code in a few places, especially where it reads the model response and where it handles tools or function calls.

question: Why do the course notebook examples need changes when I switch from Responses to chat completions with Gemini?
answer_llm:
Because the course notebook examples were written for the newer **Responses API** style, but Gemini via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint in these examples is using **chat completions** instead.

That changes a few things in the code:

- **How you call the model**
  - Responses API: `client.responses.create(...)`
  - Gemini/OpenAI-compatible chat: `client.chat.completions.create(...)`

- **How you read the answer**
  - Responses API: `response.output`
  - Chat completions: `response.choices[0].message.content`

- **How tools/function calls are handled**
  - The tools schema and call flow may need adapting to what Gemini expects.

So the notebook examples need changes because the API shape and response format are different.

question: How do I install Ollama on macOS, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
To install Ollama:

- **macOS**: Go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download), download the **`.pkg`** file, and install it.
- **Windows**: Go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download), download the **`.msi`** file, and install it.
- **Linux**: Run this in your terminal:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installation, you can test it with:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

This will download the model and start Ollama locally.
answer_orig:
First, install Ollama by visiting [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download) and choosing your operating system:

- **macOS**: Download the `.pkg` and install it.
- **Windows**: Download the `.msi` and install it.
- **Linux**: Run the following command in the terminal:

  ```bash
  curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
  ```

Once installed, open a terminal and type:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

This command will:

- Download the LLaMA 3 model (~4GB).
- Start the model locally.
- Open a chat-like interface where you can type questions.

To test the Ollama local server, run the following command:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434
```

You should receive a response similar to:

```json
{"models": [...]}  
```

Then, install the Python client with:

```bash
pip install ollama
```

Here is a minimal Python example:

```python
import ollama

response = ollama.chat(
    model='llama3',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": your_prompt}]
)

print(response['message']['content'])
```

question: Why does tiktoken give me a smaller token count than the OpenAI API for the same chat prompt?
answer_llm:
`tiktoken` can give a smaller number because the OpenAI API counts extra internal chat-formatting tokens that `tiktoken.encode()` does not include.

In particular:

- each chat message adds about **4 structural tokens**,
- the API adds **2 global tokens** to signal assistant generation,
- and whitespace/newlines/unusual Unicode can add a few more.

So the visible prompt may look like 320 tokens in `tiktoken`, while OpenAI reports 327 for the full chat request.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: What extra tokens are added by chat.completions.create that make OpenAI’s count higher?

Each `chat.completions.create()` message adds:

- **4 structural tokens per message**: role, content, separators
- **2 global tokens** for the assistant response start

So the extra tokens are the **internal chat-formatting tokens** added by OpenAI: **4 per message plus 2 overall**.

question: How can I make my API key load automatically whenever I open the project, without exporting it again in every new terminal?
answer_llm:
Use **dirdotenv** so your API key loads automatically when you enter the project directory.

Steps:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv
```

Add this to your `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`:

```bash
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh
```

Then in your project:

```bash
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically whenever you `cd` into the project folder.

If you already use it, `direnv` is also fine.
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

question: How do I decide on field weights in minsearch without just guessing, and what’s the best way to check if one setting is actually better?
answer_llm:
The systematic way is **not** to guess. For `minsearch` field weights, you should:

1. **Create a small set of representative questions**
2. **Label which documents should be retrieved** for each question
3. **Try different field weight settings**
4. **Compare them with retrieval metrics** like:
   - hit rate
   - precision@k
   - recall@k
   - MRR

That’s the best way to tell whether one setting is actually better: **evaluate against a ground-truth dataset** and compare the metrics.

For small projects, trial and error can work, but the more reliable approach is proper evaluation.
answer_orig:
The systematic approach is to evaluate different weight settings against a ground-truth dataset.

For example:

1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Mark which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different field weights.
4. Compare retrieval metrics such as hit rate, precision@k, recall@k, or MRR.

You can tune weights by trial and error for small projects, but evaluation is the more reliable approach. The course covers this topic more directly in the evaluation module.

question: What’s the best way to clean docs before using them in RAG?
answer_llm:
Clean it so it is **clean, structured, and easy to chunk and retrieve**.

Common steps:
- Remove obvious noise like broken HTML, duplicate text, boilerplate, OCR errors, repeated headers, and repeated footers.
- Preserve useful context like titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, and Q&A structure.
- Store it in a structured format if helpful, often JSON.
- Chunk it so related context stays together.
- Keep useful metadata for later filtering or ranking.

The goal is not just shorter text, but **more accurate retrieval**.
answer_orig:

- Preserve useful context such as titles, section names, dates, page numbers, speaker names, and Q&A structure.
- Store the result in a structured format that is easy to process. JSON is often convenient, but it is not mandatory.
- Chunk the documents in a way that keeps related context together.
- Keep metadata that may help filtering or ranking later.

The exact format depends on the source data. The goal is not just to make the text shorter, but to make retrieval more accurate.

question: Why does uv warn that it failed to hardlink files in GitHub Codespaces, and is that something I need to fix?
answer_llm:
No, it’s usually not an error. In GitHub Codespaces, `uv` may not be able to hardlink files from its cache into the target environment, so it warns and then falls back to copying the files instead. The package still installs correctly.

question: Why does WSL2 say the model needs more memory than available when my laptop clearly has enough RAM?
answer_llm:
WSL2 is only using the memory allocated to WSL, not all the RAM on your laptop. So even if your computer has enough total RAM, WSL2 may still report that the model needs more memory than is available inside WSL.

To fix it, increase WSL2’s RAM limit:

1. Create a file named `.wslconfig` in:
   `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

2. Add something like:
   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL:
   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Check the available memory inside WSL with:
   ```bash
   free
   ```

If you want, I can also help you choose a safe memory value for your machine.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

question: My Codespace is stuck on "Finishing up" and never connects — what's the quickest thing I should try first?
answer_llm:
First thing to try: go to **github.com/codespaces**, **stop the codespace**, then **start it again**.

If it still gets stuck on “Finishing up,” try opening it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or use a different browser.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: If GitHub Codespaces won't open in VS Code desktop, is there another way to get into it without deleting everything?

  - `uv sync`
  - `uv add ...`

If your setup seems gone after a restart, it’s usually a Codespaces environment issue, not that your project vanished. If you want, I can also tell you how to check whether your files are still there.

question: What happens to files and changes if I stop a codespace, rebuild it, or create a new one?
answer_llm:
- **Stopping a codespace:** your **uncommitted changes stay in the codespace VM** and should still be there when you start it again.
- **Rebuilding a codespace:** it starts from a **clean image**, so **system/global installs outside your project may disappear**. Your repo in `/workspaces` remains, but you may need to reinstall dependencies.
- **Creating a new codespace:** it also starts **clean**, so anything not **committed and pushed** can be lost.

In short: **commit and push often**. Uncommitted changes usually survive a stop/start, but can be lost if you **delete, recreate, or rebuild** the codespace.

question: Is OpenAI required for the RAG module, or can I plug in another LLM provider and keep the same course code?
answer_llm: No, OpenAI is not required for the RAG module. You can plug in another LLM provider and keep the course code, as long as your provider supports an OpenAI-compatible endpoint or you adapt the code a bit for differences like API keys, base URL, response format, and token usage.
answer_orig:

```

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

So in short: change the endpoint, key, and model name; if needed, also adjust provider-specific parameters like `max_output_tokens`.

question: What exactly counts as a tool or function in agentic RAG?
answer_llm:
In this course, a **tool/function** is anything the model can **call to get external help**.

Examples include:

- **searching documents**
- **querying a database**
- **calling an API**
- **running a calculation**

So it’s not about a strict technical category — it’s basically any callable capability the model can invoke when it needs something beyond its own generation.

In **agentic RAG**, the key idea is that the model can **decide when to call** the tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval usually happens as a fixed step before answering.
answer_orig:
In the context of this course, tools and functions are closely related terms. Do not worry too much about the naming difference.

A tool/function is something the model can call when it needs external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, calling an API, or running a calculation.

The important idea is that in agentic RAG, the model can decide when to call a tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: How is tool use in agentic RAG different from the fixed retrieval step in basic RAG?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, the model can **decide when to call a tool/function** based on what it needs. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a **fixed step done before** the model generates the answer.

So the difference is:

- **Basic RAG:** retrieve first, then answer
- **Agentic RAG:** the model can choose to retrieve or use other tools during the process as needed

question: Why do I get AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output' when I try OpenAI Responses API with Mistral or Groq?
answer_llm:
Because the OpenAI **Responses API** (`client.responses.create(...)` and `response.output`) is **OpenAI-specific**.  

When you use Mistral, Groq, or another non-OpenAI provider, their client often returns a different object — sometimes just a **string** — so trying to access `.output` causes:

`AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'`

Use the **chat completions** API instead for those providers:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You may also need to adapt the tools schema to the provider’s expected format.
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

Why do I get a 404 in toyaikit when I try Groq or another non-OpenAI model, and what should I switch to instead?
answer_llm:
Because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` use OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`), which is **OpenAI-only**. Groq and other non-OpenAI providers don’t implement that endpoint, so you get a `404`.

Switch to the **chat completions** classes instead:

- `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
- `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

These use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint, which Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

If you’re using Anthropic, switch to:

- `AnthropicClient`
- `AnthropicMessagesRunner`
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

question: What’s the difference between the Responses runner and the chat completions runner in toyaikit, and why does one fail with Groq?
answer_llm:
The difference is:

- **Responses runner** (`OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient`) uses OpenAI’s **Responses API**: `responses.create(...)`
- **Chat completions runner** (`OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner` / `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`) uses the older, more widely supported **chat completions API**: `chat.completions.create(...)`

Why one fails with Groq:

- **Groq does not implement OpenAI’s Responses endpoint**, so the Responses runner can return a `404` or similar error.
- Groq **does support the OpenAI-compatible chat completions endpoint**, so the chat completions runner works if you point the OpenAI client at Groq’s base URL.

So, for Groq (and other non-OpenAI providers), use the **chat completions runner**, not the **Responses runner**.

question: Why do I get a 402 from OpenRouter when I call responses.create(), even though the same code works with OpenAI directly?
answer_llm:
Because OpenRouter checks the **maximum possible output** against your key/credits before running the model, and `responses.create()` can default to a very large output allowance. If you don’t set a reasonable `max_output_tokens`, OpenRouter may reject the request with **402** even though the same code works directly with OpenAI.

Fix: set a lower limit, for example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

So the difference is mainly that **OpenRouter enforces billing/quota differently** than OpenAI, which more typically returns **429** for insufficient quota.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: In the Module 1 homework, when they say RAG should point to the chunk index, does that mean I should search over the split text instead of the full docs?
answer_llm:
Yes — it means you should search over the **split chunks**, not the full original docs.

So in Module 1 homework, the RAG index should be built on the chunked documents, e.g.:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not on:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

Then the retriever returns the most relevant chunks, and only those chunks go into the prompt. This is what reduces the context sent to the model.

It means you should build and search the index using the chunked documents, not the original full documents.

For example, if your original records are in `documents` and your split records are in `chunks`, fit the search index on `chunks`:

```python

```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

Then the RAG pipeline retrieves relevant chunks and puts only those chunks into the prompt. This is what reduces the amount of context sent to the model.

question: For the homework, do I need to get the exact same number as the multiple-choice option, or is the nearest one okay?
answer_llm: Choose the closest option. Small differences are expected, so the exact number does not have to match perfectly.
answer_orig:
Not always. If your numeric answer is close to one of the options, choose the closest option.

Small differences can come from:

- Slightly different filtering.
- Different dataset versions.
- Floating-point or rounding differences.
- Different model/provider behavior.

If your answer is far from every option, re-check the question, the dataset version, and the GitHub homework instructions.

See the general homework guidance: [homework logistics](https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/zoomcamp-logistics/homework/).

question: How can I find the token count for Module 1 homework if I'm not using OpenAI?
answer_llm:
For Module 1 homework, if you’re using a non-OpenAI provider, get the token count from the model response object’s usage fields.

For example, check fields like:

- `response.usage.input_tokens`
- `response.usage.prompt_tokens`

depending on the API style.

If your provider doesn’t expose usage, use that provider’s native tokenizer as an approximation. Do not use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for other providers.

For the current Module 1 homework, get the token count from the model response object.

For example, OpenAI-compatible clients usually return usage information on the response, such as `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`, depending on the API style.

If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check the provider's response object for its usage fields and adapt the code. Do not use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers because tokenization differs by model.

If your provider does not expose token usage, use that provider's native tokenizer as an approximation. For multiple-choice homework questions, choose the closest option.

question: Why does the grader say my repo link is not 200, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
The grader says the repo link is not 200 because it tries to open your submitted URL and expects an HTTP `200` response. If it gets `404`, `500`, or anything else, it treats the repo as not publicly reachable.

Check these first:

1. **Is the repo public?**  
   Private repos usually won’t work.

2. **Did you submit the plain repo URL?**  
   Use something like `https://github.com/you/repo`  
   not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`

3. **Is the URL typed exactly right?**  
   Even a small typo can cause a non-200 response.

Quick test: open the same link in an **incognito/private browser window**. If it loads there, it should work for the grader too.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do people use them?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are a way to turn text or other non-numeric data into numbers while keeping the meaning and context.

Why people use them in vector search:
- Similar inputs get similar vectors.
- That lets us compare items mathematically by distance or similarity.
- It makes semantic search possible, where results are based on meaning, not just exact keywords.

In short: embeddings let computers represent meaning as vectors so we can search and match by similarity.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. When similar non-numerical data is input into an embedding algorithm, it should yield similar numerical data. The proximity of these numerical values allows for the use of mathematical semantic similarity algorithms. Related concepts include the "vector space model" and "dimensionality reduction."

question: How do embeddings turn text or other non-numerical data into numbers without losing the meaning?
answer_llm: Embeddings convert non-numerical data into numerical data **while preserving meaning and context**. Similar pieces of text or other inputs should produce **similar numerical vectors**, so their **proximity in vector space** reflects semantic similarity. This lets you compare meaning with mathematical similarity methods.

question: Why do similar inputs usually end up with similar vectors in an embedding model?
answer_llm:
Because embedding models are trained so that semantically similar inputs land near each other in vector space.

The idea is:

- the model converts input into numbers,
- it learns patterns from lots of data,
- and it preserves meaning and context,
- so similar texts or items produce vectors that are close together.

That closeness makes it possible to measure semantic similarity with things like cosine similarity.

question: How does the distance between embedding values help with semantic similarity search?
answer_llm:
Distances between embedding values help semantic similarity search because **similar texts end up with embeddings that are close together in vector space**.

In practice:

- **Small distance / high similarity** → texts are semantically related.
- **Large distance / low similarity** → texts are less related.
- With **cosine similarity**, we measure how aligned two embedding vectors are, so closer vectors mean more similar meaning.

This is what lets vector search find documents that are semantically similar to a query, even if they don’t share the exact same words.

question: What’s the connection between embeddings, vector space models, and dimensionality reduction?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are the practical output of a vector space model: they convert text or other non-numerical data into numeric vectors so that similar items end up close together in space.

Dimensionality reduction is part of the idea because the original data is compressed into a smaller number of meaningful dimensions while preserving semantic information as much as possible.

So the connection is:

- **Vector space model**: represents items as vectors in a mathematical space.
- **Embeddings**: the learned numeric vectors produced by that representation.
- **Dimensionality reduction**: the process of mapping high-dimensional raw data into a lower-dimensional vector that still captures meaning.

In short, embeddings are a modern form of vector space modeling with dimensionality reduction built in.

question: Why did the lesson just use dot product for similarity without normalizing first?
answer_llm:
Because in the lesson the embeddings were already assumed to be normalized to unit length, so the dot product was effectively the same as cosine similarity.

In the homework, the vectors are not normalized by default, so you need to normalize them first if you want dot product to equal cosine similarity.
answer_orig:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used under the assumption that the embeddings returned by the model (e.g., `model.encode(...)` from OpenAI) are already normalized to have unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity.

In the homework, however, we use classic embeddings like TF-IDF + SVD, which are not normalized by default. This means that the dot product does not represent cosine similarity unless we manually normalize the vectors.

If they are **not** normalized, then you need to normalize them first for dot product to match cosine similarity.

question: For vector search in this module, do I need to embed the question, the answer, or both?
answer_llm:
There’s no single right answer — it’s an experiment on your dataset.

The course suggests trying:
- **Embed the answer (`text`) only**
- **Embed the question only**
- **Embed `question + " " + text`**

Then pick whichever gives the best retrieval quality on your ground-truth set, such as **hit rate** or **MRR**.

If you want, I can also explain when each option tends to work best.
answer_orig:
There's no single right answer — it's an experiment to run on your dataset. The course shows three options:

- Embed the answer (`text`) only — works because the model captures semantic similarity between questions and their answers.
- Embed the question only — works because user queries look like the indexed questions.
- Embed `question + " " + text` — often the best, but produces longer input and slightly more cost.

Pick whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set. The course materials include a side-by-side comparison.

question: How does cosine similarity measure how close two vectors are in text search?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures how close two vectors are by looking at the **angle between them**, not their raw size. In text search, if two document vectors point in a similar direction, they have similar content, so their cosine similarity is high.

From the context:
- **1** = vectors are identical
- **0** = vectors are orthogonal, meaning no similarity
- **-1** = vectors point in opposite directions

So in practice, it tells you how well the query vector aligns with each document vector.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: Why do people use cosine similarity for comparing documents instead of just raw word counts?
answer_llm:
People use cosine similarity because it measures the **direction/alignment** of the document vectors rather than their **size**.

Raw word counts can be misleading: a longer document may have bigger counts just because it has more words, not because it is more similar. Cosine similarity helps compare documents based on their **content distribution** by focusing on the angle between vectors, which makes it useful for text analysis.

This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: What does a cosine similarity score of 1, 0, or -1 actually mean?
answer_llm:
A cosine similarity score of:

- **1** means the vectors are **identical in direction** — they point the same way.
- **0** means the vectors are **orthogonal** — they are unrelated/no similarity.
- **-1** means the vectors are **completely opposite** — they point in opposite directions.

question: In vector search, why is the angle between two vectors more useful than their length?
answer_llm:
Because in vector search we usually care about **direction, not magnitude**.

- The **angle** between vectors tells you how similar their meanings are.
- The **length** can vary for reasons that aren’t relevant to similarity, like document length or overall scale.

That’s why **cosine similarity** is used: it measures the cosine of the angle between two vectors, so vectors that point in the same direction are considered similar even if their lengths differ.

question: Why does cosine similarity turn into a dot product once the embeddings are normalized?
answer_llm:
Because once vectors are **normalized to unit length**, cosine similarity

\[
\cos(\theta)=\frac{u \cdot v}{\|u\|\|v\|}
\]

simplifies to just

\[
u \cdot v
\]

since \(\|u\|=\|v\|=1\). So the normalization removes the denominator, and cosine similarity becomes the dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: If all vectors have unit length, why can we compute cosine similarity with matrix multiplication?
answer_llm:
Because if the vectors are unit length, cosine similarity is just the dot product:

- Cosine similarity = dot product / (norms)
- If each vector has norm 1, the denominator is 1

So for normalized embeddings, cosine similarity between a query and many documents becomes:

```python
X.dot(v_query)
```

That’s why matrix multiplication works: it computes all those dot products efficiently in one operation.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: What’s the reason cosine similarity ignores vector magnitude after normalization?
answer_llm: Because after normalization each vector has unit length, so magnitude is no longer a factor. Cosine similarity then depends only on the angle between vectors, and it becomes equivalent to the dot product.

question: How does normalizing the query and document embeddings make similarity search faster?
answer_llm:
Normalizing the query and document embeddings makes similarity search faster because, once all vectors have unit length, **cosine similarity becomes the dot product**.

That means you can compute the similarity between the query and all documents with a **single matrix multiplication** instead of doing more complex per-vector calculations. Matrix multiplication is highly optimized, so this is much faster.

question: In vector search, why is cosine similarity just the dot product between embeddings and the query?
answer_llm:
Because the embeddings and the query are normalized to unit length.

Cosine similarity measures the angle between two vectors, and when both vectors have length 1, the cosine similarity becomes exactly the dot product:

- **cosine similarity = dot product / (||a|| · ||b||)**
- if **||a|| = ||b|| = 1**, then  
  **cosine similarity = a · b**

That’s why in vector search we can compute it efficiently as a matrix multiplication between the document embeddings and the query vector.

question: If I’m doing Module 2 in Codespaces, should I keep my changes there or commit them somewhere first?
answer_llm:
Keep your changes in the repo, but **commit and push them regularly**.

For Module 2, you can use a **new codespace**, and since you may delete or recreate it later, uncommitted changes could be lost. Committing makes sure your work is stored in your GitHub repository.
answer_orig:
Just a new codespace. A codespace is an environment (see *Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?*); you create it from your existing repository, so you don't need a new GitHub repo.

Use a separate codespace for Module 2 because the vector-search dependencies are fairly heavy. Keeping them isolated means you can simply stop or delete that codespace when you're done, rather than leaving the extra weight in your Module 1 environment. Setup is quick.

Since you may delete this codespace later, commit your work to your repository (see *What happens to code saved in Codespaces if I do not commit it?*).

question: Is a codespace basically just the environment for the module, or is it the same thing as creating a new repository?
answer_llm:
A codespace is basically the environment for the module, not the same thing as creating a new repository.

You create the codespace from your existing repository, so for Module 2 you just need a new codespace, not a new GitHub repo.

question: Why does download.py stay stuck at 0% when it tries to fetch model.onnx from HuggingFace?
answer_llm:
This happens because the connection to HuggingFace’s CDN is slow or blocked.

Fix: download the file directly in your browser from:

https://huggingface.co/Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2/resolve/main/onnx/model.onnx

Then save it as:

`models/Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2/model.onnx`

The script checks whether the file already exists locally, so once you place it there, it should skip the download and continue.

question: Why do the loop and matrix dot-product versions of vector search not match exactly?
answer_llm:
They don’t match exactly because of **floating-point precision**.

`X.dot(v_query)` uses optimized low-level BLAS routines, while the for-loop adds the products in a different order. Since floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

So the values are effectively the same, just not bit-for-bit identical. Use a tolerance check like:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: Is it normal that `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` returns false even when both methods should do the same thing?
answer_llm:
Yes — that’s normal.

`X.dot(v_query)` and the for-loop version do the same math, but floating-point additions happen in a different order, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places. Because of that, `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` may return `False` if the default tolerance is too strict.

Try comparing with a tolerance, for example:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

The values are not “wrong”; it’s just floating-point precision.

question: For the Module 2 homework cosine similarity question, why do I get a value that doesn't match any of the multiple-choice answers?
answer_llm:
The most likely reason is that you used a different embedding model than the homework expects.

For Module 2 Q2, you should use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py`:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

If you used a model from the lesson like `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` or `all-mpnet-base-v2`, your cosine similarity can differ and not match any multiple-choice option.

Also double-check that:

- you embedded the page’s `content` field,
- you compared it to the query vector from Q1,
- and you used the normalized vectors from `Embedder` correctly.

If your result is still slightly off, pick the closest option.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is using a different embedding model than the homework specifies. Homework 2 tells you **not** to use `sentence-transformers` and to use the lightweight ONNX `Embedder` from `embedder.py` instead:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up Gemini so Kestra can use it in the flows and in the AI Copilot?
answer_llm:
Set up **both** environment variables, then restart Kestra:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # for the AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)            # for Kestra flows
docker compose up -d
```

Notes:
- The **AI Copilot** uses the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
- The **flows** use `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` and it must be **base64-encoded**.
- Make sure you export them in the same shell session before starting Kestra.
- If it still fails, verify the key is set correctly and consider generating a fresh one in Google AI Studio.

Inside flows, reference it like this:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```
answer_orig:
Kestra reads secrets from environment variables that are prefixed with `SECRET_` and whose value is **base64-encoded**. You export them in the terminal *before* starting Kestra. For Gemini you need two variables — the plain one (used by the AI Copilot) and the base64-encoded `SECRET_` one (used by the flows):

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then start (or restart) Kestra so it picks up the variables:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Why is Kestra not seeing my API keys unless I base64-encode them and restart Docker Compose?
answer_llm:
Because Kestra only loads secrets from environment variables that:

- start with `SECRET_`
- are base64-encoded
- are present when Kestra starts

So if you set the key after Docker Compose is already running, or if it isn’t encoded correctly, Kestra won’t see it until you restart the container.

Use this pattern:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
docker compose up -d
```

And inside flows, reference it like:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

If you want, I can also explain the difference between the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` and `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.

question: Why does Kestra AI Copilot keep saying it can only help with making Kestra flows, and what usually causes that message?
answer_llm:
Kestra AI Copilot says it can only help with creating Kestra flows when it doesn’t get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to that canned refusal.

The usual cause is that the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` is missing, mistyped, or not available in the shell/container when Kestra starts. In the Open Source edition, the Copilot specifically needs the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY`, not the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` that flows use.
answer_orig:

Make sure you exported the plain key before starting Kestra, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still fails, the key is usually missing, mistyped, or rate-limited:

- Confirm the variable is actually set in the shell you ran `docker compose up` from (`echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`).
- Generate a fresh key in [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/app/apikey).
- If you've been running the agent/multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota (`429 Resource Exhausted`) — wait a minute and retry.

Note that the Copilot needs the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` while the flows need `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` — export both.

question: Could this problem be from a bad key, a missing export in my shell, or hitting Gemini quota, and how do I tell?
answer_llm:
Yes — it could be any of those three, and the context gives a few ways to distinguish them:

- **Bad/missing Gemini key**
  - The Copilot needs the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
  - Kestra flows need the **base64-encoded** `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`.
  - If the Copilot says it can only assist with Kestra flows, that usually means it did **not** get a valid plain Gemini key.
  - Check in the same shell you used to start Kestra:
    ```bash
    echo $GEMINI_API_KEY
    ```
  - If it’s empty, missing, mistyped, or you used the wrong variable, that’s the issue.

- **Missing export / Kestra not restarted**
  - The variables must be exported **before** starting Kestra.
  - After changing them, restart Kestra so it picks them up:
    ```bash
    docker compose up -d
    ```
  - If you changed env vars but didn’t restart, Kestra may still be using the old state.

- **Bad base64 secret for flows**
  - If the flow fails with an invalid/missing key, the context says a common cause is **corrupted base64**.
  - Use `echo -n` and quote the key when encoding:
    ```bash
    export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
    ```
  - Also make sure the flow references it **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:
    ```yaml
    {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
    ```

- **Gemini quota / rate limit**
  - If you’ve been running the agent or multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota.
  - The context mentions a `429 Resource Exhausted` error as a sign of this.
  - In that case, wait a minute and retry.

So in practice:

1. Run `echo $GEMINI_API_KEY` to confirm the plain key exists.
2. Make sure `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` was created with `echo -n` and base64.
3. Restart Kestra after exporting.
4. If you see `429 Resource Exhausted`, it’s likely quota/rate limiting.

If you want, I can help you build a quick checklist to debug it step by step.

question: Why does Kestra say my Gemini key is missing or invalid even though I already set `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`?
answer_llm:
Kestra usually says that because the secret value is wrong, not because the variable name is wrong.

The most common issue is a bad base64 encoding caused by a trailing space or newline when you created `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`. Use `echo -n` and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also check these points:

- Restart Kestra after exporting the variable:
  ```bash
  docker compose up -d
  ```
- Make sure you set the variables in the same shell session you use to start Kestra.
- In your flow, reference it without the `SECRET_` prefix:
  ```yaml
  {{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
  ```

If you’re using the AI Copilot specifically, it does **not** read `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`; it needs the plain `GEMINI_API_KEY` too.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Why do we need an orchestrator like Kestra instead of just running everything in a notebook?
answer_llm:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows usually need more than code that runs once.

An orchestrator like Kestra helps with:

- scheduling workflows
- retries on failure
- monitoring and logging
- secret management
- reliably chaining multiple tasks together

So the main reason is production-style reliability and automation, not just running code interactively in notebook cells.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: Why am I getting `JSONDecodeError: Expecting value` when I try to parse the LLM output as JSON in the evaluation notebook?
answer_llm:
The LLM output is probably **not pure JSON**. It may be wrapped in a markdown code fence like ```json ... ``` or include extra explanatory text, so `json.loads(response)` fails right away with:

`JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)`

To fix it:

- Force structured JSON output with the API, e.g. `response_format={"type": "json_object"}`
- Make the prompt explicit, for example:

```text
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

Then parse:

```python
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

If you want, I can also show how to make the notebook robust against code fences before parsing.
answer_orig:
The LLM sometimes wraps the JSON in a markdown code fence or adds prose around it, so `json.loads(response)` fails with:

```
JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)
```

Force JSON output with OpenAI's `response_format`:

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

Also be explicit in the prompt about the expected shape:

```
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

Most providers have an equivalent (Gemini's `response_mime_type="application/json"`, Groq's `response_format`, etc.).

question: How do I make the model return only valid JSON instead of code fences or extra text when generating ground-truth questions?
answer_llm:
Use OpenAI’s `response_format` to force JSON, and also instruct the model very explicitly in the prompt.

Example:

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

Prompt example:

```text
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

If you’re using another provider, use their JSON mode equivalent, such as Gemini’s `response_mime_type="application/json"`.

response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(

    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)

```

Also be explicit in the prompt about the expected shape:

```

Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

Most providers have an equivalent (Gemini's `response_mime_type="application/json"`, Groq's `response_format`, etc.).

question: Why does my Jupyter notebook kernel die when I try to embed the ground-truth data?
answer_llm:
Your Jupyter kernel is probably dying because the machine runs out of RAM when the embedding model is loaded together with the rest of the notebook state.

Common fixes:

- Use a smaller embedder, like `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (384-dim)
- Run the embedding step in a separate Python script, save the vectors, then load them into the notebook
- Use a machine type with more RAM in Codespaces, or run locally
- Embed the ground-truth set in batches and free memory between batches with `del` and `gc.collect()`

If you want, I can help you choose the best workaround for your setup.
answer_orig:
Small-RAM machines (Codespaces default, low-end laptops) run out of memory when an embedding model is loaded alongside the rest of the notebook state.

Workarounds:

- Switch to a smaller embedder. `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (384-dim) is a common drop-in. Note: switching models will change your hit-rate / MRR numbers, so re-run the eval after the switch.
- Move the embedding step into a separate Python script that you run from the terminal, then load the saved vectors back into the notebook.
- Use a Codespaces machine type with more RAM (Settings → "Machine type" on a Codespace), or run locally.
- Process the ground-truth set in batches and free memory between batches (`del`, `gc.collect()`).

question: Why does adding `time.sleep(4)` still not stop me from getting rate-limited when I generate the ground-truth dataset with Gemini?
answer_llm:
Because Gemini’s free tier has **both per-minute and per-day quotas**.  

`time.sleep(4)` only helps with the **per-minute** limit, but if your `tqdm` loop runs long enough, you can still exceed the **daily** quota in a single run and get rate-limited anyway.

If that happens, the context suggests these workarounds:
- use `gpt-4o-mini` on OpenAI,
- use Groq’s free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`),
- lower thread-pool concurrency,
- and save progress so you can resume after hitting limits.
answer_orig:
Free-tier Gemini limits both per-minute and per-day requests. Adding `time.sleep(4)` only fixes the per-minute side — a long `tqdm` loop can still blow through the per-day quota in one run.

Options when this happens:

- Spend ~$5 on OpenAI and use `gpt-4o-mini`. It's cheap enough to embed/generate the entire ground-truth set and has higher rate limits.
- Use Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) — generous request-per-minute limits.
- Lower concurrency for thread-pool calls. Use a smaller pool size (2–3 workers) instead of pushing the API hard.
- Resume from where you stopped. Save progress periodically (e.g. dump the partial results to a JSONL file) so a hit limit doesn't lose all work.

```ini
POSTGRES_HOST=localhost
```

question: How do I make Pandas show the full text in a column instead of cutting it off?
answer_llm:
Use:

```python
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', None)
```

This tells Pandas not to truncate text in columns.
answer_orig:
By default, Pandas truncates text content in a column to 50 characters. To view the entire explanation provided by the judge LLM for a non-relevant answer, use the following instruction:

```python
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', None)
```

- **Option:** `display.max_colwidth`
- **Type:** `int` or `None`
- **Description:** Sets the maximum width in characters of a column in the representation of a pandas data structure. When a column overflows, a "..." placeholder is used in the output. Setting it to 'None' allows unlimited width.
- **Default:** 50

Refer to the [official documentation](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/options.html) for more details.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **Description:** Sets the maximum width in characters of a column in the representation of a pandas data structure. When a column overflows, a "..." placeholder is used in the output. Setting it to 'None' allows unlimited width.
- **Default:** 50

Refer to the [official documentation](https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/options.html) for more details.

question: Can the capstone be done as a team, or does every student need to submit their own project?
answer_llm:
The capstone is **individual** — every student needs to submit their **own project**. 

You can work in a group for the competition if you want, but **the capstone itself is not a team project**.
answer_orig:
No, the capstone is an individual project.

You can collaborate or discuss a larger idea with other students, but each submitted project must stand on its own. A shared system can work only if it is clearly decomposed into independent projects, where each person has a separate qualifying component and a separate repository.

If the work cannot be evaluated independently for each participant, it does not satisfy the project requirement.

question: Do I need to turn in two capstone projects, or is one enough?
answer_llm:
You only need to submit **one project**.

“Attempt 1” and “Attempt 2” mean you can **resubmit the same project** if needed:
- **Attempt 1**: first submission
- **Attempt 2**: improved resubmission if the first one doesn’t pass

If you want to submit **two different projects** for extra experience, that’s optional, but they must use **different datasets and problem statements**.
answer_orig:
You only need to submit one project. If the submission at the first attempt fails, you can improve it and re-submit during the attempt#2 submission window.

- If you want to submit two projects for the experience and exposure, you must use different datasets and problem statements.
- If you can’t make it to the attempt#1 submission window, you still have time to catch up to meet the attempt#2 submission window.

Remember that the submission does not count towards the certification if you do not participate in the peer-review of three peers in your cohort.

question: Do I actually need Airflow, Mage, or Kestra for the capstone, or is a simple Python script enough?
answer_llm:
No — you do **not** need Airflow, Mage, or Kestra for the capstone.

A **plain Python script** that ingests and indexes your data is enough to get full points on the **“ingestion pipeline”** criterion. A Jupyter notebook with the same steps would work too, but it’s worth **1 point instead of 2**.

Use an orchestrator only if it genuinely fits your project, such as for **recurring daily ingestion**. Don’t add one just to satisfy the criterion.

question: How can I test a recommender RAG when I don't have any real question-answer ground truth?
answer_llm:
If you don’t have real question-answer ground truth, you can evaluate a recommender-style RAG in two ways:

1. Synthetic ground truth  
   For each item in your dataset, ask an LLM to generate about 5 user queries that should retrieve that item as the top result. Then run those queries through your retriever and measure hit rate, MRR, or NDCG.

2. LLM-as-a-judge  
   Sample queries, run the full RAG, and have an LLM score the result for relevance/usefulness using a fixed rubric, like a 1–5 scale.

For ranking/recommendation problems, NDCG is often a better metric than hit rate because multiple answers may be acceptable and it rewards putting good items near the top.
answer_orig:
Two complementary approaches that both score for the evaluation criterion:

1. Synthetic ground truth (same idea as the course, adapted). For each item in your dataset, prompt the LLM with the item's description and ask it to generate ~5 user queries that should return that item as the top result. Then run those queries through your retrieval and measure hit rate / MRR / NDCG.

2. LLM-as-a-judge for end-to-end quality. Sample queries, run the full RAG, and have an LLM rate the result for relevance/usefulness on a fixed rubric (e.g. 1–5 scale, with criteria you specify in the prompt).

NDCG is often a better fit than hit-rate for ranking-style problems where multiple items are acceptable answers — it rewards getting good items high in the list, not just first.

question: What's a good way to create synthetic evaluation data for a retrieval system that recommends items?
answer_llm:
A good approach is to use **synthetic ground truth**:

1. Take each item in your dataset.
2. Prompt an LLM with the item’s description.
3. Ask it to generate about **5 user queries** that should retrieve that item as the top result.
4. Run those queries through your retrieval system.
5. Evaluate using metrics like **hit rate, MRR, or NDCG**.

For recommender-style retrieval, **NDCG** is often especially useful because it rewards good items ranked near the top, not just the single first hit.

question: For ranking-type RAG, should I use hit rate or NDCG, and why?
answer_llm:
For ranking-type RAG, use **NDCG** if you care about the **order of results**, especially when **more than one item can be acceptable**.

Why:
- **Hit rate** only tells you whether a relevant item appeared somewhere in the top-k.
- **NDCG** rewards:
  - putting the best items **higher** in the ranking,
  - and having **multiple good items** near the top.

So for ranking-style problems, **NDCG is often a better fit than hit rate**. Use hit rate if you only care about “did we retrieve at least one good result?”, but use NDCG when ranking quality matters.

question: For a huge PDF corpus, what chunking approach should I start with so I’m not overthinking it?
answer_llm:
Start simple: use fixed-size chunks of about 1000 tokens with some overlap, then run a small ground-truth eval.

Don’t try to perfect the chunker upfront. After that, iterate:
- try LLM-based splitting into logical sections,
- optionally add a short summary per chunk,
- for long structured PDFs, consider hybrid search like BM25 + dense.

The key is to measure improvement on hit rate / MRR after each change.
answer_orig:
Don't try to find the perfect chunker upfront — iterate.

- Start simple: fixed-size chunking (~1000 tokens with some overlap) and run a small ground-truth eval.
- Try smart chunking: ask an LLM to split each document into logical sections, then index each section.
- Add a short LLM-generated summary per chunk and index it alongside, or use it to boost retrieval.
- For long, structured documents (legal, financial), prefer hybrid search (BM25 + dense) so exact wording isn't lost during semantic matching.

Useful tools for parsing PDFs to clean markdown before chunking:

- [`pymupdf4llm`](https://pypi.org/project/pymupdf4llm/) — fast, decent quality.
- [Docling](https://github.com/docling-project/docling) — slower but higher quality on tables/figures.
- [GROBID](https://github.com/kermitt2/grobid) — for academic papers, extracts structure (sections, refs, etc.).

Run the eval again after each change. The goal is measurable improvement on hit rate / MRR for your ground-truth set, not a "perfect" chunker in the abstract.

question: Should I just use fixed-size chunks first, or is it better to ask an LLM to split the docs into sections right away?
answer_llm:
Start simple: use fixed-size chunking first, then evaluate.

The recommended approach is:

- begin with fixed-size chunks, around 1000 tokens with some overlap
- run a small ground-truth eval
- only then try smarter chunking, like asking an LLM to split docs into logical sections

So no, you don’t need to use an LLM to split the docs right away. The course suggests iterating and measuring improvement rather than trying to find the perfect chunker upfront.

question: Would adding a short summary to each chunk actually help retrieval, and if so how would I use it?
answer_llm:
Yes — adding a short summary per chunk can help retrieval.

How to use it:
- Generate a brief LLM summary for each chunk.
- Index that summary together with the chunk text, or use the summary as an extra field to boost retrieval.
- Then search over the chunk index as usual, but let the summary improve matching.

This is especially useful when:
- chunks are long or noisy,
- the wording in the query is a bit different from the exact text,
- you want a higher-level description to help retrieval.

The course context also suggests measuring it: run your ground-truth eval again and see whether hit rate / MRR improves.
